agent_output: {"executive_summary":"Orchestration-based saga for a courier marketplace order flow spanning 4 services (Order, Inventory, Payment, Courier). A central Saga Orchestrator drives the forward steps sequentially and, on any failure, executes compensating transactions in reverse order. This avoids distributed transactions while guaranteeing eventual consistency and clean rollback semantics.","architecture":{"components":[{"name":"Saga Orchestrator","responsibility":"Drives the order saga state machine: executes forward steps sequentially, detects failures, triggers compensating transactions in reverse order. Persists saga state for crash recovery.","boundaries":"Owns saga state and step sequencing. Does NOT own any domain data. Communicates with services via async commands/events or synchronous API calls with idempotency keys."},{"name":"Order Service","responsibility":"Creates and manages order records. Forward action: create order (PENDING). Compensating action: cancel order (CANCELLED). Publishes OrderCreated / OrderCancelled events.","boundaries":"Owns the Order aggregate (order ID, line items, status lifecycle). No knowledge of inventory, payment, or courier logic."},{"name":"Inventory Service","responsibility":"Manages stock levels and reservations. Forward action: reserve items. Compensating action: release reservation. Publishes ItemsReserved / ReservationReleased events.","boundaries":"Owns the Reservation aggregate (reservation ID, SKU, quantity, status). Reservations are time-bounded with a TTL as a safety net."},{"name":"Payment Service","responsibility":"Processes customer charges. Forward action: authorize + capture payment. Compensating action: refund/void payment. Publishes PaymentCharged / PaymentRefunded events.","boundaries":"Owns the Payment aggregate (payment ID, amount, status, idempotency key). Integrates with external payment gateway."},{"name":"Courier Service","responsibility":"Assigns a courier to fulfill the order. Forward action: assign courier. Compensating action: unassign courier. Publishes CourierAssigned / CourierUnassigned events.","boundaries":"Owns the Assignment aggregate (assignment ID, courier ID, order ID, status). May interact with courier availability/matching engine."},{"name":"Saga State Store","responsibility":"Durable persistence of saga instances: current step, payload, compensation log, retry counts. Enables crash recovery — orchestrator can resume or compensate incomplete sagas on restart.","boundaries":"Append-only log per saga instance. Accessed only by the Saga Orchestrator."}],"interfaces":[{"from":"Saga Orchestrator","to":"Order Service","contract":"CreateOrder(orderId, customerId, items) → OrderCreated | OrderFailed; CancelOrder(orderId) → OrderCancelled","coupling_type":"async command/event via message broker (or sync API with idempotency key)"},{"from":"Saga Orchestrator","to":"Inventory Service","contract":"ReserveItems(orderId, items) → ItemsReserved | ReservationFailed; ReleaseReservation(orderId) → ReservationReleased","coupling_type":"async command/event via message broker"},{"from":"Saga Orchestrator","to":"Payment Service","contract":"ChargeCustomer(orderId, customerId, amount, idempotencyKey) → PaymentCharged | PaymentFailed; RefundPayment(orderId, paymentId) → PaymentRefunded","coupling_type":"async command/event via message broker"},{"from":"Saga Orchestrator","to":"Courier Service","contract":"AssignCourier(orderId, deliveryAddress) → CourierAssigned | AssignmentFailed; UnassignCourier(orderId, assignmentId) → CourierUnassigned","coupling_type":"async command/event via message broker"}],"data_flow":"1. Client → Saga Orchestrator: PlaceOrder command. 2. Orchestrator persists saga (step=ORDER_CREATE). 3. Orchestrator → Order Service: CreateOrder → receives OrderCreated, advances to step=INVENTORY_RESERVE. 4. Orchestrator → Inventory Service: ReserveItems → receives ItemsReserved, advances to step=PAYMENT_CHARGE. 5. Orchestrator → Payment Service: ChargeCustomer → receives PaymentCharged, advances to step=COURIER_ASSIGN. 6. Orchestrator → Courier Service: AssignCourier → receives CourierAssigned, saga COMPLETED. On FAILURE at any step N: Orchestrator reverses through steps N-1…1, issuing compensating commands. Each compensation acknowledgment advances the rollback pointer. When all compensations complete, saga status = COMPENSATED.","patterns_used":[{"pattern":"Orchestration Saga","rationale":"Central orchestrator provides clear visibility into saga state, simplifies debugging and monitoring compared to choreography. The 4-step sequential flow with ordered rollback maps naturally to a state machine."},{"pattern":"Compensating Transaction","rationale":"Each service exposes an explicit undo operation. This is the fundamental mechanism for maintaining consistency without distributed transactions."},{"pattern":"Idempotent Operations","rationale":"Every command carries an idempotency key (saga ID + step). Services deduplicate on this key, making retries safe after network failures or orchestrator crashes."},{"pattern":"Saga State Machine","rationale":"Each saga instance is modeled as a finite state machine with well-defined transitions. States: ORDER_CREATE → INVENTORY_RESERVE → PAYMENT_CHARGE → COURIER_ASSIGN → COMPLETED (forward) and COMPENSATING_PAYMENT → COMPENSATING_INVENTORY → COMPENSATING_ORDER → COMPENSATED (backward)."},{"pattern":"Outbox Pattern","rationale":"Services publish events via a transactional outbox (write domain change + event in one local transaction, relay asynchronously). Prevents lost events when the service commits but the broker is unreachable."}]},"domain_model":{"bounded_contexts":[{"name":"Order Management","classification":"core","aggregates":["Order (orderId, customerId, items[], status: PENDING|CONFIRMED|CANCELLED, timestamps)"]},{"name":"Inventory","classification":"supporting","aggregates":["Reservation (reservationId, orderId, items[{sku, qty}], status: RESERVED|RELEASED, expiresAt)"]},{"name":"Payment","classification":"generic","aggregates":["Payment (paymentId, orderId, customerId, amount, status: AUTHORIZED|CAPTURED|REFUNDED|VOIDED, idempotencyKey)"]},{"name":"Courier Assignment","classification":"core","aggregates":["Assignment (assignmentId, orderId, courierId, status: ASSIGNED|UNASSIGNED|IN_TRANSIT|DELIVERED)"]},{"name":"Saga Coordination","classification":"supporting","aggregates":["SagaInstance (sagaId, orderId, currentStep, stepLog[], status: RUNNING|COMPENSATING|COMPLETED|COMPENSATED|FAILED, createdAt, updatedAt)"]}],"context_map":[{"upstream":"Saga Coordination","downstream":"Order Management","relationship":"Customer-Supplier (Orchestrator commands, Order Service fulfills)"},{"upstream":"Saga Coordination","downstream":"Inventory","relationship":"Customer-Supplier"},{"upstream":"Saga Coordination","downstream":"Payment","relationship":"Customer-Supplier with ACL (Payment Service wraps external gateway behind its own model)"},{"upstream":"Saga Coordination","downstream":"Courier Assignment","relationship":"Customer-Supplier"}]},"trade_offs":[{"decision":"Orchestration over Choreography","gains":"Single place to see saga state. Easier to debug, monitor, and add steps. Explicit rollback ordering. Simpler to reason about compensation sequences.","sacrifices":"Orchestrator is a coordination single point — must be highly available. Slightly more coupling (orchestrator knows all participants). Services aren't fully autonomous in triggering next steps.","alternatives_considered":["Choreography-based saga: each service emits events and the next service reacts. Rejected because 4-step sequential flow with ordered compensation is hard to trace in pure choreography, and debugging becomes a distributed puzzle."]},{"decision":"Async messaging over synchronous HTTP calls","gains":"Temporal decoupling — services can be independently deployed and scaled. Natural retry/dead-letter semantics. Resilient to transient failures.","sacrifices":"Added infrastructure complexity (message broker). Eventual consistency rather than immediate. Harder to return a synchronous response to the caller.","alternatives_considered":["Synchronous REST calls with circuit breakers. Viable for low-scale, but fragile under load and harder to guarantee delivery."]},{"decision":"Persisted saga state store","gains":"Crash recovery — orchestrator can restart and resume or compensate any in-flight saga. Full audit trail of every step.","sacrifices":"Write amplification — every step transition is a write. Storage costs. Slightly higher latency per step.","alternatives_considered":["In-memory saga state with event sourcing replay. More complex to implement, but viable at higher scale."]}],"flexibility_points":[{"area":"Adding new saga steps","evolution_path":"The state machine is a sequence. Adding a step (e.g., Fraud Check between Payment and Courier) requires: 1) define forward + compensating action, 2) insert new state in the machine, 3) deploy. No changes to existing services."},{"area":"Switching from async to sync per-step","evolution_path":"Each step's communication is behind an adapter interface. Can swap messaging for HTTP per-service without changing orchestrator logic."},{"area":"Parallel step execution","evolution_path":"If future steps are independent (e.g., notification + analytics), the state machine can fork into parallel branches that join before advancing."},{"area":"Timeout and retry policies","evolution_path":"Per-step timeout and retry configuration. Can be tuned independently. Dead-letter queues catch poison messages."}],"rigidity_points":[{"area":"Compensation ordering","reason":"Compensations MUST execute in reverse order of the forward steps. This is a hard invariant — violating it (e.g., refunding before releasing inventory) can cause data inconsistencies or double-spend."},{"area":"Idempotency contract","reason":"Every service command MUST be idempotent. This is non-negotiable for correctness under retries and crash recovery. Cannot be relaxed."},{"area":"Saga state transitions","reason":"The state machine transitions are strictly defined. No ad-hoc state jumps. This rigidity prevents partial or inconsistent compensations."}],"risks":[{"risk":"Orchestrator becomes a single point of failure","severity":"high","mitigation":"Deploy orchestrator as a stateless service backed by durable saga state store. Multiple instances consume from the same queue with partition-based assignment. Health checks + auto-restart."},{"risk":"Compensation itself fails (e.g., refund call to payment gateway times out)","severity":"critical","mitigation":"Retry compensations with exponential backoff. After max retries, move to FAILED state and alert ops. Implement a dead-letter compensation queue with manual intervention tooling. Never silently drop a failed compensation."},{"risk":"Saga gets stuck in COMPENSATING state indefinitely","severity":"high","mitigation":"Implement a saga timeout watchdog. If a saga remains in COMPENSATING beyond a threshold (e.g., 15 minutes), escalate to ops dashboard. Also implement reservation TTLs in Inventory Service as a safety net."},{"risk":"Message ordering or duplication causes incorrect state transitions","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Idempotency keys on all commands. Saga state machine rejects out-of-order events (only processes events matching current expected step). Outbox pattern prevents ghost messages."},{"risk":"Inventory reservation TTL expires before saga completes","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Set TTL conservatively (e.g., 10 minutes). Monitor saga step durations. If p99 approaches TTL, alert. Orchestrator can extend reservations if needed."}],"implementation_guidance":{"recommended_approach":"Implement the saga orchestrator as a state machine with explicit states: ORDER_CREATING, ORDER_CREATED, INVENTORY_RESERVING, INVENTORY_RESERVED, PAYMENT_CHARGING, PAYMENT_CHARGED, COURIER_ASSIGNING, COMPLETED, COMPENSATING_COURIER, COMPENSATING_PAYMENT, COMPENSATING_INVENTORY, COMPENSATING_ORDER, COMPENSATED, FAILED. Each state has a handler that issues a command and a transition that fires on the response event. Use a saga state table (sagaId PK, orderId, currentState, payload JSON, stepLog JSON array, createdAt, updatedAt) for persistence. Each service implements two endpoints: a forward action and a compensating action, both idempotent on sagaId.","files_to_create_or_modify":["saga-orchestrator/src/sagas/OrderSaga.ts — State machine definition with forward + compensation steps","saga-orchestrator/src/sagas/SagaRunner.ts — Generic saga executor: drives state transitions, handles retries, persists state","saga-orchestrator/src/sagas/SagaStateStore.ts — Repository for saga instances (DB-backed)","saga-orchestrator/src/sagas/types.ts — SagaStep, SagaState, SagaInstance interfaces","order-service/src/handlers/createOrder.ts — Forward: create order, Compensate: cancel order","order-service/src/handlers/cancelOrder.ts — Idempotent order cancellation","inventory-service/src/handlers/reserveItems.ts — Forward: reserve, Compensate: release","inventory-service/src/handlers/releaseReservation.ts — Idempotent release","payment-service/src/handlers/chargeCustomer.ts — Forward: authorize+capture","payment-service/src/handlers/refundPayment.ts — Compensate: refund/void","courier-service/src/handlers/assignCourier.ts — Forward: assign","courier-service/src/handlers/unassignCourier.ts — Compensate: unassign"],"patterns_to_follow":"Each saga step is defined as { name, forwardAction, compensatingAction, timeout, maxRetries }. The SagaRunner iterates forward steps, persisting state after each transition. On failure, it reverses through completed steps calling compensatingAction. All actions return a result type: Success(data) | Failure(reason). Use the outbox pattern in each service: write domain change + outgoing event in a single local transaction, then relay events to the broker asynchronously.","pitfalls_to_avoid":["Do NOT call compensations in forward order — always reverse","Do NOT skip saga state persistence between steps — a crash mid-saga with no persisted state means orphaned reservations and charges","Do NOT assume compensations are instant — they can fail and need their own retry logic","Do NOT use the saga orchestrator to hold business logic — it only coordinates; business rules live in the services","Do NOT forget idempotency keys — without them, retries cause double charges or double reservations","Do NOT couple the saga to a specific transport — use an adapter so you can swap messaging implementations"]},"escalations":[],"confidence":{"level":90,"high_confidence_areas":["Orchestration saga is the right pattern for this sequential, ordered-rollback flow","Compensation ordering and idempotency are well-understood requirements","The state machine model maps cleanly to the 4-step flow"],"low_confidence_areas":["Specific message broker choice (Kafka vs RabbitMQ vs SQS) — depends on existing infrastructure","Whether Payment Service should authorize-then-capture in two sub-steps or single charge — depends on payment gateway capabilities","Exact timeout values for reservations and saga steps — needs production traffic data"],"assumptions":["Each service owns its own database (database-per-service pattern)","A message broker is available or will be introduced","Services can be made idempotent on saga ID","The 4 steps are strictly sequential with no parallelism in the current flow"]}}
